ICML 2026

Use ← and → to move. Press O for overview, F for full screen, and P to print or save as PDF.

What does “understand 80%” mean?

Know most of the map—and know how to explore the rest.

Explain

Describe the main areas and trends in normal language.

Compare

Tell which evidence supports a claim and which evidence does not.

Explore

Place a new paper on the map and ask useful follow-up questions.

Five signals. Five different questions.

Paper share
How large is an area?
Not: Is every paper important?
Program signal
Which areas appear more among orals and awards?
Not: Is this a complete quality ranking?
AlphaXiv attention
What attracts attention on that platform?
Not: What is objectively best?
Venue baseline
What is more or less emphasized than nearby venues?
Not: What caused the difference?
Artifact link
Is code or another artifact visible?
Not: Can the result be reproduced?

Good understanding includes uncertainty

Checked fact

Direct count or official label

Example: the official corpus contains 6,628 paper records.

Directional pattern

Several signals agree

Useful for navigation, but paper-level review is still needed.

Open question

The evidence cannot settle it

A strong next step is more valuable than a confident guess.

The project still has 310 manual claim and area review rows to complete.

Twelve broad areas

A navigation map—not twelve sealed boxes.

  • 1. LLM reasoning and post-training
  • 2. Multimodal, vision, and perception
  • 3. Theory, optimization, and algorithms
  • 4. AI for science, health, and neuro
  • 5. Data, causality, and federated ML
  • 6. Systems and efficient models
  • 7. Safety, governance, and privacy
  • 8. Agents, code, and tools
  • 9. Graphs, geometry, and representations
  • 10. Generative modeling
  • 11. Reinforcement learning and control
  • 12. Robotics and world models

The conference is broad—but not evenly distributed

Source: current 12-area taxonomy. Area assignments still contain review boundaries.

The conference story in six moves

1. Reasoning after pretraining

Rewards, verification, search, and extra work at answer time.

2. Systems and agents

The model is only one part of a useful system.

3. Different attention signals

Program selection and public visibility do not agree.

4. Robotics visibility

A small area can attract very high public attention.

5. Venue context matters

A large area can still be underweight versus other venues.

6. Links are not reproduction

Visible artifacts are clues that still need inspection.

Technical area map

LLM Reasoning, Post-Training, and RLVR

1,099mapped papers
16.6%paper share
1.29×oral enrichment
2.03×public attention

Subareas

Reasoning models and chain-of-thought; reward modeling and preference feedback; RL with verifiable rewards; diffusion language models; general LLM training and evaluation.

Common methods

GRPO and policy optimization; process or outcome rewards; test-time search and sampling; verifiers; preference learning; alternative decoding orders.

What gets evaluated

Mathematics and code accuracy; pass@k; reward-model calibration; reasoning coverage; compute-performance tradeoffs.

Main fault line

A high score on cheaply verifiable tasks may not transfer to open-ended reasoning, and broad LLM training papers can inflate the area boundary.

LLM Reasoning, Post-Training, and RLVR

The Flexibility Trap: Rethinking the Value of Arbitrary Order in Diffusion Language Models

Outstanding Paper · oral

Problem

Arbitrary-order diffusion language models have more flexible decoding paths, but that flexibility may avoid uncertain tokens needed for exploration.

Method

Compare arbitrary-order and autoregressive reasoning coverage, then apply standard GRPO while retaining parallel diffusion decoding.

Evidence

Mathematics and coding evaluations, pass@k coverage, matched RL comparisons, and 89.1% reported GSM8K accuracy.

Main result

In the tested reasoning settings, simpler autoregressive RL elicits stronger reasoning than preserving arbitrary token order.

Limit to remember

The conclusion is task-dependent; arbitrary ordering can still help constraint-satisfaction or non-reasoning tasks.

ICML paper page · Technical summary is based on the local abstract and review workspace.

Technical area map

Multimodal, Vision, and Perception

889mapped papers
13.4%paper share
0.58×oral enrichment
0.80×public attention

Subareas

Vision-language reasoning; cross-modal alignment; video and motion; 3D and spatial understanding; robustness and adversarial perception.

Common methods

Vision-language post-training; multimodal transformers; temporal modeling; 3D geometry; video diffusion; grounding and attribution.

What gets evaluated

Image and video reasoning; spatial consistency; temporal dynamics; localization; robustness under corruptions and distribution shift.

Main fault line

Models can answer through language priors without using visual evidence, while aggregate vision labels hide large differences between video, 3D, and robustness work.

Multimodal, Vision, and Perception

Motion Attribution for Video Generation

Outstanding Paper Honorable Mention · oral

Problem

Video generators improve quickly, but it is unclear which fine-tuning clips cause better or worse motion rather than merely changing appearance.

Method

Motive uses gradient-based data attribution with motion-weighted loss masks to isolate temporal influence from static appearance.

Evidence

Text-to-video fine-tuning, VBench motion measures, data-curation experiments, and a reported 74.1% human-preference win rate over the base model.

Main result

High-influence clips selected by motion attribution improve temporal consistency, physical plausibility, smoothness, and dynamic degree.

Limit to remember

Attribution depends on the chosen motion loss and model; human preference does not fully establish causal or physical correctness.

ICML paper page · Technical summary is based on the local abstract and review workspace.

Technical area map

Theory, Optimization, and Algorithms

737mapped papers
11.1%paper share
1.45×oral enrichment
0.46×public attention

Subareas

Statistical learning theory; online learning and bandits; convex and nonconvex optimization; Bayesian methods; transformer theory; numerical methods.

Common methods

Generalization bounds; regret analysis; stochastic optimization; spectral analysis; probabilistic inference; expressivity and impossibility results.

What gets evaluated

Proof assumptions and rates; synthetic experiments; sensitivity to dimension, data, compute, and hyperparameters; empirical checks of predicted behavior.

Main fault line

A theorem can be correct yet rely on assumptions or scales that do not describe current production models.

Theory, Optimization, and Algorithms

To Grok Grokking: Provable Grokking in Ridge Regression

Outstanding Paper Honorable Mention · oral

Problem

Why can a model fit its training data early but generalize only much later?

Method

Analyze over-parameterized ridge regression trained by gradient descent with weight decay and derive quantitative grokking-time bounds.

Evidence

End-to-end proofs of overfitting, delayed generalization, and eventual low error; hyperparameter studies; nonlinear neural-network checks.

Main result

Grokking delay changes predictably with training conditions and can be amplified or removed through hyperparameter choices.

Limit to remember

The rigorous result is for classical linear regression; nonlinear experiments support transfer but do not prove the same mechanism for large models.

ICML paper page · Technical summary is based on the local abstract and review workspace.

Technical area map

AI for Science, Health, and Neuro

587mapped papers
8.9%paper share
1.08×oral enrichment
0.33×public attention

Subareas

Proteins and molecules; physical science and climate; time series and forecasting; neural dynamics; spiking and biological signals.

Common methods

Graph and geometric networks; scientific foundation models; diffusion and flow; equivariant architectures; uncertainty modeling; simulators and surrogates.

What gets evaluated

Scientific validity; physical constraints; calibrated uncertainty; out-of-distribution generalization; laboratory or domain-expert verification.

Main fault line

Benchmark improvement may not translate into a valid molecule, useful forecast, clinical decision, or new scientific finding.

AI for Science, Health, and Neuro

Protein Autoregressive Modeling via Multiscale Structure Generation

Oral

Problem

Protein backbones have structure at several scales, while single-scale generation can miss the relationship between global topology and atomic detail.

Method

PAR combines multiscale downsampling, an autoregressive transformer for next-scale conditioning, and a flow-based decoder for backbone atoms.

Evidence

Unconditional backbone generation, zero-shot conditional generation, motif scaffolding, design-quality measures, and scaling analysis.

Main result

Coarse-to-fine autoregression produces high-quality backbones and supports flexible conditioning without task-specific fine-tuning.

Limit to remember

Structural validity is not biological function; data overlap, physical plausibility, and laboratory performance still require direct checks.

ICML paper page · Technical summary is based on the local abstract and review workspace.

Technical area map

Data-Centric, Causal, and Federated ML

526mapped papers
7.9%paper share
0.75×oral enrichment
0.64×public attention

Subareas

Data quality and labels; continual learning; causal discovery and inference; federated learning; distributed and heterogeneous clients.

Common methods

Replay and rehearsal; data valuation and selection; causal graphs and interventions; federated aggregation; debiasing; task adaptation.

What gets evaluated

Forgetting and forward transfer; intervention accuracy; client heterogeneity; privacy and communication cost; label efficiency and robustness.

Main fault line

Predictive gains do not establish causality, and simplified client or task sequences may hide real deployment constraints.

Data-Centric, Causal, and Federated ML

Pretrained Vision-Language-Action Models are Surprisingly Resistant to Forgetting in Continual Learning

Oral

Problem

Robot policies must learn new skills without destroying old ones, but continual-learning knowledge mostly comes from smaller models trained from scratch.

Method

Compare large pretrained VLA models with smaller behavior-cloning policies under sequential tasks and simple experience replay.

Evidence

Forgetting, forward learning, replay-buffer size, retained knowledge, and recovery after fine-tuning across robot-policy tasks.

Main result

Pretraining changes continual-learning dynamics: small replay buffers can produce little or no forgetting while retaining fast adaptation.

Limit to remember

The result may depend on the pretrained model, task similarity, replay access, and chosen robotics sequence; taxonomy placement also crosses into robotics.

ICML paper page · Technical summary is based on the local abstract and review workspace.

Technical area map

Systems and Efficient Foundation Models

515mapped papers
7.8%paper share
0.69×oral enrichment
1.31×public attention

Subareas

Large-scale training; optimizers and architectures; model compression; quantization; KV-cache and long context; inference and serving.

Common methods

Parallel optimization; spectral constraints; low precision; sparsity and MoE; cache pruning; kernels; speculative decoding; systems co-design.

What gets evaluated

End-to-end throughput and latency; memory; training stability; energy or cost; quality regression; results across hardware, batch sizes, and sequence lengths.

Main fault line

An isolated kernel speedup may disappear end to end, and efficiency can trade away reasoning, calibration, or safety.

Systems and Efficient Foundation Models

Controlled LLM Training on Spectral Sphere

Oral

Problem

Large-model optimizers can control update scale while weights and activations still drift, weakening width-invariant stability guarantees.

Method

The Spectral Sphere Optimizer constrains module weights and updates, derives steepest descent on the spectral sphere, and implements it in Megatron.

Evidence

Pretraining Dense 1.7B, MoE 8B-A1B, and 200-layer DeepNet models against AdamW and Muon; activation, outlier, and router-balance analysis.

Main result

SSO reports faster or better optimization with bounded activations, suppressed outliers, and improved MoE load balance.

Limit to remember

The claim needs matched compute and hardware accounting, broader model families, and end-to-end cost analysis beyond optimizer stability.

ICML paper page · Technical summary is based on the local abstract and review workspace.

Technical area map

Safety, Governance, Privacy, and Society

502mapped papers
7.6%paper share
1.41×oral enrichment
0.51×public attention

Subareas

Adversarial safety and security; alignment and oversight; privacy; fairness and social effects; governance and evaluation practice.

Common methods

Red teaming; probes and monitors; adversarial training; privacy attacks and defenses; causal audits; human studies; policy analysis.

What gets evaluated

Attack success and adaptive evasion; false positives; distribution shift; realistic threat models; downstream harm; subgroup and privacy measures.

Main fault line

A narrow detector benchmark may not represent real risk, and training against a monitor can teach a model to evade it.

Safety, Governance, Privacy, and Society

The Obfuscation Atlas: Mapping Where Honesty Emerges in RLVR with Deception Probes

Outstanding Paper Honorable Mention · oral

Problem

Training against white-box deception detectors may produce honest behavior—or teach a deceptive model to hide from the detector.

Method

Use a coding reward-hacking environment, deception probes, RL penalties, KL regularization, and a taxonomy of honest and obfuscated outcomes.

Evidence

Probe scores, representation drift, task return, policy text, RL comparisons, and a policy-gradient argument for detector-evading behavior.

Main result

Representation drift can produce obfuscated activations; detector penalties can incentivize obfuscated policies; sufficiently high KL plus penalty yields honest policies in this setting.

Limit to remember

The coding environment and selected detector do not cover every form of deception, monitoring, or real-world agent behavior.

ICML paper page · Technical summary is based on the local abstract and review workspace.

Technical area map

Agents, Code, and Tool Use

496mapped papers
7.5%paper share
1.19×oral enrichment
1.52×public attention

Subareas

Agent evaluation; tool use; code generation and verification; software tasks; multi-agent planning, search, and coordination.

Common methods

Planning and reflection; tool routing; memory; execution feedback; multi-agent roles; verifiers; environment and benchmark design.

What gets evaluated

Task success; pass rates; tool errors; cost and latency; recovery from failure; contamination; realistic environment coverage; safety boundaries.

Main fault line

Better instructions, tools, or scaffolding can look like learned intelligence, while benchmark success may depend on fragile environment details.

Agents, Code, and Tool Use

PaperBanana: Automating Academic Illustration for AI Scientists

High public attention · artifact visible

Problem

Publication-ready research figures require reference search, content planning, rendering, and repeated design revision.

Method

Orchestrate specialized VLM and image-generation agents for retrieval, planning, rendering, and iterative self-critique.

Evidence

PaperBananaBench with 292 NeurIPS 2025 method diagrams; comparisons on faithfulness, conciseness, readability, aesthetics, and plot generation.

Main result

The full agentic pipeline reports consistent gains over leading diagram-generation baselines.

Limit to remember

The evaluation may favor curated academic styles; repository, benchmark release, unusual-domain behavior, and component ablations need checking.

ICML paper page · Technical summary is based on the local abstract and review workspace.

Technical area map

Graphs, Geometry, and Representation Learning

391mapped papers
5.9%paper share
0.61×oral enrichment
0.38×public attention

Subareas

Graph neural networks; geometric representation learning; manifolds; equivariant networks; relational and structured latent representations.

Common methods

Message passing; permutation equivariance; invariant and equivariant layers; spectral graph methods; geometric priors; contrastive and self-supervised objectives.

What gets evaluated

Generalization across graph size; algorithm execution; molecular and physical validity; symmetry tests; expressivity and oversmoothing.

Main fault line

Fitting small graph instances does not prove an architecture learned an algorithm that generalizes to arbitrary size.

Graphs, Geometry, and Representation Learning

Which Algorithms Can Graph Neural Networks Learn?

Oral

Problem

Message-passing GNNs often imitate algorithms empirically, but it is unclear when finite training examples imply correct behavior on larger inputs.

Method

Develop necessary conditions for learning algorithms with MPNNs, prove generalization properties, establish impossibility results, and derive stronger variants.

Evidence

Theorems covering shortest paths, minimum spanning trees, dynamic programming, and 0–1 knapsack, plus limits for tasks standard MPNNs cannot compute.

Main result

The framework separates algorithms learnable from small instances from tasks requiring more expressive message-passing architectures.

Limit to remember

Formal learnability conditions may require idealized data, optimization, precision, or architecture assumptions not guaranteed in practice.

ICML paper page · Technical summary is based on the local abstract and review workspace.

Technical area map

Generative Modeling

379mapped papers
5.7%paper share
0.83×oral enrichment
0.96×public attention

Subareas

Diffusion sampling and inverse problems; flow models; image and video generation; controllable generation; sampling theory and consistency.

Common methods

Score matching; denoising diffusion; flow matching; transport; guidance; distillation; random matrix analysis; efficient sampling.

What gets evaluated

Sample quality and diversity; likelihood or bounds; speed; consistency across seeds and data splits; controllability; human preference; physical plausibility.

Main fault line

Media-quality metrics, sampling theory, and reproducibility questions are different goals and should not be collapsed into one ranking.

Generative Modeling

A Random Matrix Perspective on the Consistency of Diffusion Models

Outstanding Paper Honorable Mention · oral

Problem

Diffusion models trained on different data splits can produce surprisingly similar samples from the same noise seed. Why?

Method

Build a random-matrix framework for finite-data effects on linear denoisers and sampling maps, including expectations, variance, and sampling trajectories.

Evidence

Closed-form predictions for linear models and empirical validation on UNet and DiT architectures in a non-memorization regime.

Main result

Shared Gaussian statistics explain much cross-split consistency; disagreement depends on spectral anisotropy, input inhomogeneity, and dataset size.

Limit to remember

The sharp theory is linear and Gaussian; nonlinear modern generators may depart from it through feature learning, memorization, or conditioning.

ICML paper page · Technical summary is based on the local abstract and review workspace.

Technical area map

Reinforcement Learning and Control

312mapped papers
4.7%paper share
0.76×oral enrichment
0.66×public attention

Subareas

Core and offline RL; online learning and exploration; policy optimization; robust RL; control; model-based learning and planning.

Common methods

Value and policy learning; actor-critic; offline objectives; model-free planning; regret analysis; robust optimization; compute-adaptive policies.

What gets evaluated

Return and sample efficiency; generalization across horizons; offline-to-online transfer; robustness; safety; compute and parameter budgets.

Main fault line

LLM-facing RL is only one part of the field, and reward or benchmark design can dominate the apparent improvement.

Reinforcement Learning and Control

On Computation and Reinforcement Learning

Oral

Problem

Standard RL architectures mix parameter count with computation, making it hard to ask whether a fixed-size policy benefits from more compute.

Method

Formalize compute-bounded policies, prove capability and horizon-generalization separations, and build a variable-compute minimal architecture.

Evidence

Theory plus 31 online and offline RL tasks, comparing extra compute with feed-forward and residual policies using up to five times more parameters.

Main result

A fixed-parameter policy can solve harder and longer-horizon tasks by spending more computation at decision time.

Limit to remember

The chosen tasks and architecture may not capture wall-clock latency, environment interaction cost, or complex continuous-control deployments.

ICML paper page · Technical summary is based on the local abstract and review workspace.

Technical area map

Robotics, Embodiment, and World Models

195mapped papers
2.9%paper share
0.81×oral enrichment
2.11×public attention

Subareas

Vision-language-action policies; manipulation; robot memory; latent actions; embodied planning; world models and physical prediction.

Common methods

VLA pretraining; behavior cloning; policy learning; memory modules; latent dynamics; video and action representations; simulation and real-robot transfer.

What gets evaluated

Long-horizon success; memory and recovery; occlusion; real versus simulated robots; task diversity; robustness; safety and latency.

Main fault line

Visually impressive demos and project pages can attract attention while hiding narrow tasks, resets, manual intervention, or weak generalization.

Robotics, Embodiment, and World Models

RoboMME: Benchmarking and Understanding Memory for Robotic Generalist Policies

Oral · high public attention

Problem

VLA memory mechanisms are evaluated in narrow, inconsistent settings, limiting comparison on history-dependent manipulation.

Method

Create 16 tasks across temporal, spatial, object, and procedural memory, then compare 14 memory-augmented variants on a π0.5 backbone.

Evidence

Long-horizon tasks involving repeated actions and occlusion, with controlled comparisons of memory representations and integration strategies.

Main result

No single memory design wins everywhere; effectiveness depends strongly on the task and type of memory required.

Limit to remember

Benchmark coverage, real-world transfer, base-policy dependence, and the currently mismatched repository metadata all need further inspection.

ICML paper page · Technical summary is based on the local abstract and review workspace.

Reasoning and post-training

16.6%

1,099 mapped papers—the largest current area.

Pretraining is no longer the whole story

  • Learn from rewards and preferences.
  • Check intermediate work and final answers.
  • Spend extra computing time on hard questions.
  • Explore alternatives to left-to-right generation.
Be careful: broad LLM training papers can be mistaken for reasoning papers.

The surrounding system is becoming central

515

Systems and efficient models

Memory, serving, compression, caching, hardware, and end-to-end cost.

496

Agents, code, and tools

Planning, execution, memory, tool environments, and software tasks.

Ask: did the model learn more—or did better scaffolding make the system look smarter?

Program selection and public attention disagree

Theory and safety lean toward program signal. Robotics and LLM reasoning lean toward public attention in this snapshot.

Two areas that public attention can hide

Theory, optimization, and algorithms

1.45×oral enrichment
0.46×public attention

Mathematical depth may be harder to see in a fast public feed.

Safety, governance, and society

1.41×oral enrichment
0.51×public attention

Technical safety contributions can matter without becoming popular demos.

Area enrichment helps choose what to read. It does not rank every paper.

Robotics and world models

2.9%

The smallest paper share among the 12 areas.

2.11×

The highest public-attention enrichment.

A careful conclusion: robotics receives unusually high AlphaXiv attention for its size.
Not supported: robotics is the best or most important area.

Large inside ICML. Underweight versus nearby venues.

Multimodal and vision: 889 papers, 13.4% of ICML, yet about 3.1 percentage points below the project’s nearby-venue baseline.

A repository link is the start of a question

1. Identity

Does the link belong to this paper?

2. Contents

Are code, data, weights, and instructions present?

3. Execution

Can it run in a clean environment?

4. Result

Do the outputs match the paper?

Visible ≠ runnable ≠ reproduced.

Read every paper through five questions

1. Problem

What is the paper trying to change or understand?

2. Contribution

What does it add?

3. Evidence

What supports the claim?

4. Limit

Where might the conclusion fail?

5. Wider meaning

Why does it matter for the conference map?

Four papers that make the map concrete

Reasoning
The Flexibility Trap

Tests whether flexible generation order actually helps diffusion language-model reasoning.

Theory
To Grok Grokking

Explains delayed generalization in a controlled mathematical setting.

Agents
PaperBanana

Uses an agentic workflow for academic illustrations—and leaves artifact questions to check.

Safety
The Obfuscation Atlas

Asks whether training against deception monitors creates honesty or better hiding.

A useful map has borders, not walls

A paper may be evidence for more than one story.

Example: Neural Thickets can be read as efficient adaptation, optimization, or post-training.

Trends contain disagreements

Scores ↔ reliability

Does a better benchmark score mean more dependable reasoning?

Capability ↔ cost

What ability is lost when a model becomes faster or smaller?

Agents ↔ safety

What happens when a capable tool user makes a mistake?

Demos ↔ evaluation

Does a striking example survive repeated and realistic tests?

Benchmarks ↔ use

Does measured success matter in the real scientific or social setting?

Links ↔ reproduction

Can another person actually run and check the result?

Check 1: What can we conclude?

Robotics has 2.9% of mapped papers and 2.11× public-attention enrichment.

A

Robotics receives unusually high AlphaXiv attention for its size.

B

Robotics is the highest-quality research area at ICML.

A is supported. B changes a platform-attention signal into a quality claim.

Check 2: A GitHub link has many stars

What should you do before calling the paper reproducible?

Minimum answer

Confirm the repository belongs to the paper and contains the promised materials.

Strong answer

Run it cleanly, compare outputs, and document missing steps or differences.

Stars show attention, not reproduction. Identity, contents, execution, and results all need checks.

Build a five-minute ICML 2026 briefing

Include

  • One sentence about the map
  • Three major trends
  • One contrast between signals
  • Two paper examples

Protect against overclaiming

  • Two important caveats
  • One open question
  • Signal names where they matter
  • No link-as-reproduction claim

Choose your next step

3–4 hours

Quick tour

Map, four trends, four papers, and a short briefing.

10–12 hours

Standard route

All lessons, 12-paper course, checkpoints, and final quiz.

About 20 hours

Deep route

Full papers, artifact inspection, venue comparison, and written synthesis.

The goal is not to finish quickly. It is to leave with a reliable way to understand new research.
